Design has a blind spot: its go-to approach to any situation is to make good things
Ever since the industrial revolution we've had unfathomably cheap, plentiful, hard-to-make things
We can't wrap our heads around this, since we have ancestral memory of finely made things being rare and precious
Therefore we have a tendency to acquire and keep things which don't actually benefit our lives
Designers generally exacerbate the problem by making more and more and finer and finer things
When what's really needed are methods to deal with the root problem of accumulating stuff crowding out our space to think
That's why Marie Kondo sparked a whole movement, but she's really just the very beginning of our culture learning to adapt to this problem
Not everyone needs to become a minimalist, but most people need to develop some counterbalancing tendency against default acquisitiveness
Are there fields within design where the default approach is to remove material?

@visakanv I've been noticing a timing element that makes it really hard to go back and write essays you've already "gotten past" in your mind.

@visakanv Everything works so much better when you're exploring a fresh question, trying out some answers to see how they sound, and then working that into a linear train of thought in real time.

In the process of writing, you've come to a conclusion which is itself a new building block.

@visakanv But if you try to go back and try to explain one of your old blocks that's already layers deep in the foundation, it sounds all wrong.  It's something about the tone: you're lecturing, not exploring.

@visakanv At that point I start questioning myself: "Who the hell am I to tell people to build their concepts the same way I do?"  

Haven't figured out a tone that conveys how speculative it all is.  But it's no longer feels speculative *to me* because I've accepted my old premise.

somehow this thread about life force conveys the idea better than Christopher Alexander's giant library of seven tomes on the subject. https://t.co/2fAh9qRliy

I've been really into Christopher Alexander for years now, I think he's great, I believe in his cause.  But the very way he describes evaluating the life contained in a structure objectively ... it encourages you approach the whole endeavor from a kind of dead perspective.

OK, he was struggling to persuade these frigid modernist architects who didn't believe in life force at all, so he was leaning on these social science questionnaire statistical methods for credibility, and I have sympathy for his plight.
Must have been hell trying to argue against those people.

But the result is, he shows you one picture of a stool, and then another picture of a different stool, and then he demands: 

"You must choose: which one is more alive?"

And you're staring at these dismembered stools sitting there on the bare floor, and it's as if he's asking you  
Which one is more green than the other?
And then you wonder whether you're oddly colorblind to this mystical life force that he claims everyone else sees.

BUT HE'S SHOWING YOU THE MUMMIFIED CORPSE OF A STOOL!  

He's showing you a glass museum case of some ancient artifact.
He's not showing you a stool being used as a seat, or a stool being used as easel, or a stool being used as a drying rack.  Those are living stools!

Ok enough about the stools already.  My point is: this idea that you can summon the feeling of … collective house-cleaning bonhomie, 

and that you can even do it alone, 

and you can do it to help yourself do chores …
… it has this consequence that it reveals to me that I can make whatever chair is in the room become a living chair by using that same shift in perspective.
Living furniture is not some inherent quality to the furniture, it's the consequence of that furniture being used by particularly alive people.
You make the tools and objects and clothes around you alive by using them.  They take on more life by adapting to your needs.
And you don't have to wait until you've curated some perfect set of furniture that have all been evaluated for maximum aliveness.
Anyway, thanks again to @nvpkp for articulating ᴛʜᴇ ʟɪꜰᴇ ꜰᴏʀᴄᴇ in a way that got me past a certain stuckness in how I was seeing it.

@visakanv I've been grappling with this question too, of how much personal experience to include in essays.

I especially have trouble with introductions, how to bring up the main idea, and find personal stories especially good for that.

@visakanv Reading Cheryl Mendelson's "Home Comforts", I was totally hooked by her introduction where she confessed that the act of writing the book was really a sort of ... attempt to reconcile these deep family issues that haunted her in a way:

@visakanv 1. Serious conflict between the approaches of her two grandmothers, who each taught her their own culture's way of housekeeping, and scolded her for demonstrating the other's.  She was caught in a bind about this and clearly felt bad about it.

@visakanv 2. A generational level set of social expectations where the role of "housekeeper" was seen as demeaning, unimportant, passé, even shameful, among her high-achieving college and law-school peers.

it seems to me like the better solution for most people is just:  "Find a colleague who's good at marketing."

Great observation about a phenomenon that's really hard to describe in words.

I've often wondered about this too, and my favorite theory is that muscle knots are caused by feedback loops in the nerves. https://t.co/n80lh3Hc9L
One thing that struck me when studying neuromuscular anatomy was how many "relays" there are between the brain deciding to do something, and the muscle (or "set of motor units") firing.

It's like, brain tells spinal cord, spinal cord tells nerve, nerve tells muscle group, muscle group branches into like 50 parts, each part branches into 100 fibers, each fiber branches into 100 of spindles, and each spindle needs to recieve the signal many places along its length

At each one of those steps, there are local conditions that can either dampen or amplify the signal.  

Those conditions have nothing to do with what you're "trying" to do consciously.
These local sensors influencing how the signal gets passed along are what "reflexes" are.  Current science has identified ... something on the order of 10-20 of them?  There could be more.

Ok I was way off there have to be over 50 here:
https://t.co/Km8uOw7jFQ

But a lot of them are specific to particular parts of the body that don't really develop knots, so those aren't the ones I'm talking about.

The ones I suspect are relevant to muscle knots are things like:

Stretch Reflex
Golgi Tendon Reflex
Arthrokinetic Reflex
Withdrawal Reflex

The fact that these are even named suggests we know more about them than we do.  A lot of this is medical knowledge on the order of "ABC Syndrome"

As in: "Sometimes people present behaviors A, B, and C.  Guess we'll call it ABC Syndrome."

As I understand it, there are a number of sensors in the muscle fiber and surrounding fascia.  They basically sense:

1. length
2. rate-of-change of length
3. tension

4-?. probably a whole lot of other things too, especially chemistry and metabolic energy

The stretch reflex seems to be about getting the muscle to tense up to prevent injury from a joint getting hyperextended.  

The golgi tendon reflex seems to be about making the muscle give up before it snaps a tendon powerlifting.

So they're kinda opposites.
But the way they're described, and the way they're tested (classic tapping under the kneecap), is kinda taking the whole-muscle level macro view.

What if you zoom in?

What if it's also happening not on the scale of the whole muscle tensing in order to protect a joint, but one fiber tensing up to protect an adjascent fiber?

I've seen a lot of procrastination advice lately taking the form:

"If you can't coerce yourself into doing what you claim to want, then you just need to admit to yourself that you never actually wanted it.  Forget about that and figure out what it is you genuinely want instead."

While doing some unrelated writing I stumbled across a word that seems to capture the distinction well:  "aspiration"
An aspiration is something like an ideal.  An identity you aspire to.  An identity you admire.
Certain professions actually still use this word as a formal title in the accreditation process:

"Aspirant" -- a young person on the path.  Above a Neophyte, below a Journeyman.

It's a statement of intention, not a descriptor of competence.
It's a proclamation that the Aspirant admires the skills and values of the tradition/profession/role/masters.

Unfortunately, such clear paths are scarce these days, and people are left in the absence of structures, having to invent career paths by themselves.  Hence the tide of personal-productivity-anti-procrastination advice.
I guess I'm just saying: you don't need to let go of your aspirations every time you see yourself NOT making direct progress towards them.
You can aspire to be the kind of person who runs a clean efficient kitchen, while still not wanting to do the dishes right now.
That's not a contradiction of terms.  You don't need to force yourself to give up on one of those desires, or the other.

Just holding the aspiration will ... I don't know ... somehow make things easier.  It will subtly help you notice possibilities in that direction, even if you don't seem to be able to follow the right procedures to get there.

The thing creative people tend to forget when complaining about imagination deficits how *insanely faster* we can get things done when we're following scripts and using blinders.  

It can literally be the difference between a project taking a day, or taking decades.

"Imagine a room."
"Ok, easy."
"Now imagine the floor, the joists under the floor, the sill, the walls, the studs, the plaster, the rafters, and the roof.  The sheathing, siding, insulation, the shingles."
"Uhhh, okayyy..."
"Now, can you build it?"

In a sense what it means to be a carpenter is to have internalized a set of blinders that prevent you from imagining all the ways a structure could be made.  

You need to stop imagining possibilites and start cutting and nailing boards.
The decisions are the most time-consuming step of any project.  Or maybe, they're the only part that could expand forever, if imagination isn't effectively constrained.

The thing about living in ... civilization is that your entire environment has been made by experts

so stepping outside the mediated environment is really demoralizing because not only are you indecisive without blinders, but everything you make feels crude by comparison.

We have the idea of a learning curve, where the beginning is the fun part, because you're learning so quickly.  But that's only once you've discovered some subculture to learn about.

The imagination can be an utter DESERT before you get there.

Anyone have a clear position on how OK it is to propagandize yourself?
I’m constantly trying to formulate a distinction between art and propaganda.  I don’t have a satisfying answer yet.
And I don’t think it’s necessarily bad to tell yourself stories about yourself.  There’s practically no way around that, and getting better at it is what lots of personal growth practices are about.
But there’s a flavor of it that feels like propagandizing at yourself, rather than just understanding yourself.
I notice it in tweet formulas like  “I must live a certain way”  or  “Me vs the character I am in my head”
I notice it also in people generating AI images of themselves.  It’s like they’re inserting themselves into their favorite fiction genre.
I guess what bothers me about it is … OK, once upon a time it was pretty easy to tell what propaganda was, because it came from a Government Office.
But now and for the past … 80 years or so, the bulk of propaganda has been consumer advertising, not government.  And what does come from the government gets laundered through the news media.

And so the bulk of popular fiction, if it’s not propaganda itself, has gone through a winnowing process where … incompatibilities with the consumer value system have been shaved off.  They haven’t made it past the filter.

@forshaper My take lately is that comes down to people's pricing models for their own labor.  Cost-based pricing vs "What's it worth to you?"-pricing.

@forshaper when carpenters start doing side jobs, they tend to start with cost-based pricing.  (materials + hours@wage)

It's hard to believe how much clients are willing to pay, because they're not taking into account overhead and insurance and all the stuff a real business pays for.
@forshaper the whole appeal of wage work is you just show up and do what you're told and don't have to model anyone else's ... needs and wants and negotiating position

@forshaper General point is you move into a different pricing regime once you stop seeing yourself as selling a physical product (house, loaf of bread, whatever) and start seeing yourself as selling someone a solution to their problem.

@forshaper I guess this is more a model for why management always makes more than labor, and the "happy spot" is when a new field opens up and labor supply is management's growth bottleneck, so they don't mind paying extra wages because it's a race into a new market frontier.

One of the weirdest parts of our psychology is our ability to forget about big problems we haven't solved.
I don't know how to think about this, so let me just brainstorm some angles.

1.  After we try shit for a while, and it doesn't seem to work, eventually we stop trying.  This could be seen in a positive light as learning from failure, or conserving resources.  Or in a negative light as giving up.

2.  We need to handle other day-to-day things in the meantime, and so eventually we turn our attention to those, and when it turns out our other life-strategies still work, we sort of shrug:

"maybe Big Problem wasn't so big after all, if I can still work around it."
3.  Related to the 'muddling through' frame: some problems don't get solved, they just get muddled through.
4.  Even the word "problem" might itself be a problem, but I'm kinda okay with using it for now (a perfect microcosm).

Not everything we work on should be seen as a problem, but this class of things probably should, because they're all … big, bad, maybe vague, maybe intractable … sources of future harm that we avoid thinking about.
5.  Ashcroft heads see forgetting problems as contracted awareness.

6.  Venkat had a good line I can’t track down about first-world moralism re: order-of-operations in problem solving, something like:

“A recipient of charity should not spend any money on fun until he's solved the basic boring problems."
7.  You could call it a recentness bias: we focus on what's changed recently, not what's been around for a while.
8.  You could file it under mankind's infinite capacity to take things for granted.

9.  Anyone have any sources about what the actual time horizons on this are?  Like those habit-book guys, but for how long it takes us to get used to (stop resisting) various types of environmental changes?

one that I can think of is how injury-related compensatory movement patterns take about two weeks to get established

after that, even once healed your nervous system has no reason to ever stop limping. it takes some fancy physical therapy or a lot of conscious awareness to fix
10.  Related: that phenomenon of people living right under a dam being the least concerned about it, and people living at the edge of the danger the most worried.

11.  Broad class of wicked problems: "How to diagnose maladies where causality takes decades plus?"

I wrote a little poem about it once

To understand cause and effect
separated in time
is the hardest thing.

Is that why we live so long?
To live to see effects
so many years
 removed from their causes
that we alone remember them?
Anyway I was thinking about this class of problems because one that frequently nags me is something like:
*taps mic*  we live in a surveillance society.

And, we know from history that surveillance societies don't thrive.  They choke themselves out and die, slowly, painfully, while limping down a miserable road of shame, guilt, and backstabbing.  This is the most obvious thing in the world.

But we can't seem to stop, especially because ours is not a surveillance state, it's a seemingly voluntary surveillance market.  There's no dictator to overthrow.  Except the dictator of our own preferences for convenience.
*taps mic* we live in a surveillance society, and most of it happened in our lifetimes.
*taps mic* we live in a surveillance society, and we participated in its construction.

Now, this brings us to one of the major life alignment pitfalls that lead people to madness: 

deciding to Take A Stand Against Evil.
Okay, okay, I get it, focus on what you want to see more of in the world.
Fixate on your opponents and you only give them power.
You win a struggle by formulating an ideal as precisely and vividly as possible while completely ignoring the other guy.

But like, really??  At what point does that become complicity?  At what point does that become willful blindness?

Don't problems need to be seen and recognized before they can be solved?

I have a natural sympathy for cranks, and an instinctive distrust for authority, so this is a dilemma I've been pondering my whole life:

"How can cranks get taken seriously, if they're right?"
I used to think the answer was "they need to become more articulate and persuasive", but I'm coming to believe more and more that this is a dead end.  Or at least, not adequate on its own.

All this to say: on the one path you end up a raving madman, on the other you censor yourself in order to preserve your credibility, which you can never spend on the one thing you actually care about using it for.

a lot of public craziness is driven by an inner logic of:

"Why won't they *listen* to me?  It must be because I'm not emoting hard enough."
... everyone continues ignoring them, as they gradually dial up the intensity of emotion, until it finally crosses the 'crazy' threshold ...
... at which point the inconvenient objector can be dismissed for being crazy, instead of being dismissed for being unimportant, or non-urgent, as they were previously.

Putting it this way highlights how essentially infantile this pattern is.

Which, again, maddeningly, only reinforces the dismissal of the problem.  A restatement of the Cassandra Complex I suppose.
To wrap this up with some glimmer of hope, the *taps mic* gesture is one of the best public postures I've seen towards this class of huge, slow moving clusterfuck.

Live your life as a respectable, upstanding person, and then every once in a while, like every six months or two years or something, just gently remind people that the problem   *is a problem*   without ranting too hard.
ideally at a time when some of the acute short-term symptoms of the problem are already being discussed.
This isn't likely to win you a lot of friends, because speaking up about problems without proposing solutions is not a good look.
But it will have the beneficial effect of showing others that reasonable people don't need to remain silent about the situation.  Which, for the time being, might be the best you can do.

@SpikTroll I always shill for this freehand ripping technique.  I find it so much easier on your saw and chain, because you're mostly cutting ribbons off the sidegrain.  

The saw cuts faster, and you aren't slowed down fiddling with rails, depth settings, any of that.

@SpikTroll Another advantage is you don't need two saws, one clamped in the mill and another for trimming off knots, wide points, etc.  

Way less kit to set up, and fewer little parts to get lost in the sawdust.
@SpikTroll I like a semi chisel chain filed at 5° ~ 10°.  That helps the upper par of the bar glide along the walls of an existing cut without wanting to dig into the walls.

First we were citizens
Then we were consumers
Now we are becoming viewers https://t.co/fQjM29dLpV

the identity of the citizen is predicated on the nation state

the identity of the consumer is predicated on industrial production and global trade (physical distribution)

Hypothesis:
Prussian schooling was us learning to systematically traumatize ourselves to render us better market participants.

Autism is us learning to systematically traumatize ourselves in order to render us better internet participants.
thought somewhat inspired by this two-by-two from Dror Poleg : https://t.co/yNBnTvSJCj

where I see things a bit differently is it wasn't the new *institutions* that reined in the new production capacities so much as the new *interaction protocols* -- specifically market transactions, commodification.
So if the point of Prussian Schooling was to teach us to get our needs met by performance in the marketplace, to teach us the primacy of the *market protocol* ...

... then the point of whatever's going on behind the autism phenomenon is that we're learning to get our socializing and idea-exchanging needs met via the internet, absent richer etiquettes face-to-face.
This might sound like a pretty horrifying prospect, but I think it's clear that it's a part of the future that is already here, but not widely-distributed yet.
I think it's mainly horrifying if you're starting from the premise that cultural evolution is driving towards a world that's nice for people to live in.
Think I'm finally beginning to understand what Bruce Sterling meant when he said he doesn't believe in human-centered design.

Anyway, I'd encourage everyone to read The Machine Stops, especially if you've never read it before, because that's where this "socializing via flattened metrics" is headed.

@hermittoday Well, I believe the quote was referring to pretty small-scale chemistry: thermodynamics, laminar vs. turbulent flows, molecular crystalline structure.

@hermittoday The best visual is the supersaturated solution that suddenly precipitates out when a disturbance is made and one part of it "notices" that a crystalline structure is possible.  The pattern spreads rapidly through the previously more-disordered substance.

@hermittoday In my mind, that mixes a little bit with an old alchemical perspective which is that our world consists of two opposing currents: the physical, which flows toward decay in accordance with entropy, and the biological/conscious, which tends towards order and complexity.

@hermittoday "shift the entire system to a higher order" ... the word higher here is a bit of a red herring, or a point of confusion, because there's something like a "restful state" for each perspective, and they are at odds with each other.
@hermittoday To Western Consciousness, it's more restful when things are understood as patterns, and categorized in ways we've named.

@hermittoday So my understanding of morphic fields would be rougly: "Like minds have noticed they can rest if they understand the world according to this pattern, so the pattern spreads through the population quickly, perhaps even at a distance in ways we can't explain yet."

-ecological: invasive species terraforming an environment
-cultural: ways of seeing spreading mimetically

Online publishing disrupted traditional publishing and we all suffer for it.
Traditionally your editor or your agent or someone like that provided supportive context *once you made it* into the publishing world.
Ok, you wanna be a writer?  You write some manuscripts, you send em off, you get a lot of rejection.
Finally, somehow, someone notices you and you get an editor, an agent, a publisher, or some combination of those.

At that point, your editor hounding you for pages becomes your supportive context.  Not necessarily a pleasant one, but it is a supportive context.  It's an ongoing social pressure to get you to keep writing, and it's a particular person you can keep in mind as your audience.

And it's a quality pre-filter.  

You the writer don't necessarily want to be the one evaluating what you're writing.  The Madman Architect Carpenter Judge framework explained this perfectly.

(maddeningly hidden behind JSTOR paywall, please reply if anyone has a link)

You, the writer, don't want to be evaluating the quality of your output as you're making it, that's death.  So you have an editor, who does that for you before the finished product goes out into the world.
The way that has been disrupted is now publishers are social media companies.
So you don't have to fight to get a publisher anymore, you can just start putting stuff out.

But you also don't have an editor, you don't have the quality pre-filter, and there's a new sensibility emerging that says:  "it's impossible to know what the public is gonna like, so you have to just publish everything."
You cut out the pre-filter step entirely, because publishing is free now.  You're not wasting money on ink and paper, it's all just pixels.
(seemingly free, paid for on the backend by surveillance marketing)
So there's no cost to publishing bad stuff, and you don't know whether stuff is bad or good, so you just throw everything at the internet and see what sticks.  See what goes viral.
And my vague hunch is that this is bad because the editor-author relationship *has value*.
The internet publishing model has undermined that relationship, to the point that it doesn't exist anymore, especially for new/small authors.
One new way that it is re-emerging is  "Thank you to ____, ____, and ____ for feedback on early versions of this blogpost/essay."
This gives you some of the quality pre-filter back, but it doesn't give you the supportive-context pressure, which is the part that I miss and want to reinstate.

The other thing is, an editor can ensure continuity on a big project, in a way that early readers of little blogposts can't.  Your editor keeps you on track, refocuses you when you're getting off track, and holds a years long deadline in mind sometimes.
All this is to say, I would really like to see a cultural norm develop around the resurgence of the author-editor relationship.
I think we need a way to have this relationship in the internet age.  And if we're gonna have it, I think it looks different from the publishing house model.

I think there do need to be financial stakes.  Roughly, we should come up with a contract where a writer makes a contract with an editor for an e-book or anything intended to be published and sold for money, where the editor gets 20%, or 40%, idk.  Deadline of 1-3 years, maybe.

I still believe that creative people are largely not driven by money.  And there needs to be someone who IS driven by money for creative projects with long boring parts to get FINISHED.  That's the editor.
Part of the inherent structure of the author-editor relationship is that the editor is more of a mercenary.
The author is obeying the inscrutable exhortations of their soul, channeling the muse, etc.
And the editor is the mediator between that perspective and the purely commercial perspective of: what can we sell?
The editor has more faith in the commercial viability of the work than the author does, so their role is to help the author carry on when the author is having doubts.

It's good, actually, for the editor to be financially motivated, and it's good, actually, for the editor to push the author to finish something he's no longer creatively inspired by if it actually has a market.
Every sizable creative project has stages like that, and it's better not to face them alone.
If the market wants it, it should exist.  That's the editor's premise.
I think splitting the proceeds is the right financial structure.  And regular meetings between writer and editor is the right creative rhythm.

Protecting, valorizing, re-establishing the author-editor relationship, that's the core of this thread.

I want to make an analogy to the term "bodyfat setpoint."

We have something like "internal effort setpoint."  And technology fucks with it.
Resetting one's bodyfat setpoint is mysterious and vague.  It's still a speculative theory.
But resetting one's internal effort setpoint is ... at least as mysterious and vauge.  Where does it come from?  Some combination of culture and personality I guess.
Gonna try to brainstorm some examples of phenomena that can be thought of as "internal effort setpoint miscalibrations."
-bullshit jobs or email jobs feeling bad if you can't work hard enough at them to satisfy your effort setpoint.
-blue collar workers wrecking their bodies because they learned the first day on the job to work at 100% effort.
-people not charging enough when they succeed at automating something, or when they do something with equipment that they used to do by hand.

What are some principles for operating in an environment where nobody knows how to use the tools correctly?

@_jlevers the thing I’ll say is: you’re always going to have to do work you’re not enthusiastic about, and work that doesn’t align exactly with your values.  That’s always some portion of it.

@_jlevers But when you have a sort of balanced diet between passion/idealistic/stretch/max-interestingness work and “bread and butter” work, the latter can feel like a kind of pleasant rest.  The pressure’s off, yet you’re still helping helping someone else with their problem.

@_jlevers Even if *in your view* their problem is kind of not-worth-solving, or you think they’re going about it wrong, or whatever.  You get it done and they’re happy.  That’s all it needs to be, there can be satisfaction there.

Finally reading this Christopher Lasch I've heard so much about.  Thread of highlights:
"The ideology of personal growth, superficially optimistic, radiates a profound despair and resignation.  It is the faith of those without faith."

So long as the collective prospect looked on the whole so bright, the self-made man found deferring gratification *brought* gratification, and abundant profit.  But 

"In an age of diminishing expectations, the Protestant virtues no longer excite enthusiasm."
"A profound shift in our sense of time has transformed work habits, values, and the definition of success."
"people today resent anyone who … attempts to use the past as a standard by which to judge the present."
"Henry Ward Beecher defined 'the beau ideal of happiness' as a state of mind in which 'a man [is] so busy that he does not know whether he is or is not happy.'"

“The nineteenth-century cult of success placed surprisingly little emphasis on competition.  It measured achievement not against the achievements of others but against an abstract ideal of discipline and self-denial”
"As a formula for success, winning friends and influencing people had little in common with industry and thrift."
"academic psychology retreats from the challenge of Freud into the measurement of trivia"

"This book, however, describes a way of life that is dying—the culture of competitive individualism, which in its decadence has carried the logic of individualism to the extreme of a war of all against all ...

... the pursuit of happiness to the dead end of a narcissistic preoccupation with the self.  Strategies of narcissistic survival now present themselves as emancipation from the repressive conditions of the past."
"many radical movements in the past have drawn strength and sustenance from the myth or memory of a golden age in the still more distant past.  ...

... This historical discovery reinforces the psychoanalytic insight that loving memories constitute an indispensable psychological resource … those who cannot fall back on the memory of loving relations in the past suffer terrible torments as a result."
"Instead of drawing on our own experience, we allow experts to define our needs for us and then wonder why those needs never seem to be satisfied."
"Having surrendered most of his technical skills to the corporation, he can no longer provide for his material needs.  ...

... As the family loses not only its productive functions but many of its reproductive functions as well, man and women no longer manage even to raise their children without the help of certified experts."
"Through compulsive industry and relentless sexual repression, nineteenth-century Americans achieved a fragile triumph over the id."
"But the self-preoccupation … arises not from complacency but from desperation; nor is this desperation confined to the middle class."

"The collapse of personal life originates not in the spiritual torments of affluence but in the war of all against all, which is now spreading from the lower class, where it has long raged without interruption, to the rest of society."
"social problems inevitably present themselves also as personal ones"

Every society reproduces its culture … in the individual, in the form of personality.  
"Personality is the individual socialized."  (quote is Durkheim's)

"Psychosis is the final outcome of all that is wrong with a culture"
-Jules Henry

I’ll take this as a prompt to do a thread of highlights from Bruce Sterling’s “Information Wants To Be Worthless” talk on how there has never been a functional economic basis for creative writing. https://t.co/N0pxiunnyn

The world of the gentleman-publisher never existed.  Really.  And not only did it *not exist*, but it did a lot of harm to the cause of literature because it was mostly just a myth. https://t.co/XmlUdOaVoo

What would it look like if we actually had a functional economy that was on the side of the creative artist?  What would the optimum literature-producing economics look like?  

You want to set up a system and the outcome is “Better Writing!”  What would it actually take?
He then goes on to discuss a bunch of models:
State Support: here take this money and write some literature in your local dialect.
MacArthur Genius grants: “Oh, you’re very smart.  Here take this money and go hide.”
The Nobel Prize in literature is legendary for stopping people from ever writing anything good again.

The hobbyist method:  Writer’s need a profession.    

“What you really need is a guy who’s a doctor.  Like me, Chekhov!”
“A lot of people who dedicate their lives to literature do harm to literature.”   (Bruce can never resist quoting Havel)
The pulps, which I think are great.  But there’s not a lot of slum left even if you want to go slumming.

Teaching.  The American MFA model.  

“I think this is the worst solution.  I actually think that teaching is worse for writers than drink.  … I’ve seen it ruin so many writers of promise.”

As someone who has untied literal knots in literal rope professionally for some years now, I have to nit-pick this metaphor. https://t.co/89z3KRi84l
TENSION forces *in the direction the knots are supposed to hold* are what tightens knots.
You know what you need to untie knots?  FORCE.  Big force.  Just from a direction the knot doesn't expect.
I really know only two or three basic techniques for untying knots:
1.  Put it on a flat surface and hit it with a hammer.  Change angle, repeat.
2.  Drive a marlinspike through it, or a screwdriver if that isn't available.  Once it's through, wiggle it around.  If that isn't enough, try a second one and leverage them against each other.
The big factor is patience.  It's funny to me, people will literally throw away expensive ropes because they can't get the knots out.

Since this is intended as a how-to thread about untying knots, I guess the third technique worth mentioning is to use a vise and a pair of high-leverage pliers to grab the rope in two different places and wobble.  Most abrasive and sketchy, but can work sometimes.

Prices aren't real.
What is a fair wage when prices aren't real?
Imagine the absurdity of carpenters earning royalties on their buildings.  And yet why not?  Why do we have that for intellectual property?
In order to go into business for yourself, you really need a philosophy of pricing.

The simplest one is is just to price by comparison: charge what everyone else charges for similar stuff.

That works well enough.  But overthinkers like us demand satisfaction in the form of grand unifying theory.

And as your product gets more nebulous, abstract, creative, or vibes-based, it becomes a lot less clear what your comparisons are.

Which then offers a major temptation to lie to yourself about how valuable your work is.
The aim is for every deal to feel fair to both sides.  What is a fair wage, what is a fair profit?  What is a fair mark-up, if you're retailing?
Thinking about deals, sales, and employers that have felt square to me in the past, I came up with some hallmarks:

-a good handshake, before and after the work is done
-a clear agreement, good communication about what will be done and when.  a good spec.
-speedy resolution.  lack of uncertainty.
-both sides feel the work was done correctly and skillfully
And one more: the lack of desperation.  If neither party is desperate, both parties feel like they are entering voluntarily.  Otherwise, there is potential for exploitation and resentment.
So a wage is a price, but prices aren't real.  The thing that makes "calculating the true price" impossible is:

Everything contains an infinite amount of embodied energy if you trace it back far enough.  And every piece of work you do will continue accumulating value into the future.  Even if only as a lesson about failure.
So a price is just putting a cap on that value, at a given point in time.
The price is not trying to be "true"  The price is a way to get things moving.  A way to get the energy to circulate.  A way to get people interacting.
Magic happens when people pass power back and forth.  (h/t Eric Weinstein)
Prices offer a measure of safety when doing that with strangers.  They are a trust protocol, a limit on liability.
Prices, at their best, are a way to stop looking backwards.  A way to stop haggling over grievances.
Does this strike anyone else as a kind of microeconomic version of Keynsianism?

I’ve been thinking about propaganda, and what separates art from propaganda, for a long time. https://t.co/hdSC3XSeOw
The other day I came across a couple of things on the TL that made me notice a parallel between how the terms 'Cult' and 'Propaganda' function as categories for dismissal.

The first was this thread by Tomas Pueyo, where he describes cathedrals as propaganda:
https://t.co/8SqQjCJeIs

I want to resist: “Oh, no!  It’s such tasteful propaganda!  It’s such permanent and lasting propaganda, look, it’s etched in stone!  The gargoyles will persist for centuries.  Even today we devote millions to its upkeep.  Cultural moneuments like this are high art!"

Also floating around in my head is Venkat's "Mars and the Meaning of Money" about how cathedrals and space programs are civilizational art projects, whose function isn't instrumental.  They're more like civilizational capability yardsticks.

https://t.co/j6oNSZVban https://t.co/2BRnVYaYuI

Which you might interpret as: "Civilization making propaganda for itself, to reinforce its values by proving what impressive things a society with those values can accomplish."

The point is to over-awe the spectator, as an advertisement for the creator's value system.
So in theory, I can accept the idea that cathedrals are propaganda by their nature and intent.  Their nature is to persuade by over-aweing with beauty, harmony, grandeur, splendor.

The second piece was sparked by back-and-forth I had with Odalisk on his thread about Cults.
https://t.co/ShAfMsTw0j
I was resisting his point, which I interpreted as a kind of: “Cults are bad, here’s how to spot them and avoid them.”

I resisted it based on a perspective of “everything is a cult, culture is simply the sum of all cults, normalcy is the largest cult of all.”

Which I think I also got from Ribbonfarm.
https://t.co/EBV8bncSwN

"You can't dismiss a cult simply by virtue of its being a cult--every cult must be evaluated on it's own merits!"

But this poses a problem.

@br___ian you get it
https://t.co/IfrUjAloUs

Basically, as long as you could live by the rule "Cults are bad, propaganda is misinformation,"  you had intellectual license to ignore huge swaths of the clamoring media and the dysfunctional culture.

If all culture is cults, and all creative work is propaganda, you've lost this useful heuristic.

It's not just the loss of a rhetorical tool, it's an enormous increase in your mental evaluation load.
You have to evaluate every cult on whether or not it's a *good* cult, every piece of propaganda on whether it's a *good* piece of propaganda.
Suddenly you're in the realm of trying to refute billboard bullshit that takes 10x the effort to refute as it does to create.  You're drowning.

This is pretty much as far as I've gotten here.  It's not a conceptual state of affairs I'm satisfied with.

I need some new derogatory terms here -- new broad categories I can safely ignore.  I'm open to suggestions.

@hermittoday But this kind of thing goes against *many* etiquette and leadership principles when you're in a group.  Those principles exist for good reasons.

@hermittoday It's sowing doubt, discord, and worry.
@hermittoday If you're a leader, you're drawing people's attention to a realm where you don't know what to do as a leader.  That *terrifies* people.

@hermittoday You need a really high-trust group of exclusively people who can handle a lot of uncertainty, ambiguity, and doubt.  Really, you can only do this when all their practical needs are handled beyond doubt, and limit the area of investigation to one thing at a time.
@hermittoday yes, but accepting that all people have insecurities and egos is what leadership entails.

@hermittoday You're right that this is a very real pathology in corporate cultures, but among people who do hard physical work together, complainers are powerfully shunned.  They don't get invited back.  It's a universal etiquette.

In the interest of comparing notes on how our minds operate, I'm going to try a thread about "Mechanic's Mind" and what it's like to be in it. https://t.co/xHnueYHRYy
Mechanic's mind is a way of thinking I've had naturally since I was quite young.  So it's kind of hard to notice the basic assumptions and habits of it, but here's an attempt.

OP has a natural skill at systems thinking, and mechanics mind may be somewhat similar, but it's limited to man-made systems.  

You assume that everything has a function, and then you get into the mind of the designer to figure out what that function is.

You assume that everything has a function, and also, that only *one part* not-working is what's making the whole thing not work.  

There's a causal chain, one link is broken, and it's your job to identify and fix that.

So you actually spend very little time in wide-awareness, because you very-rapidly proceed to diagnostic awareness. 

"If this part were broken, what behavior would manifest?"
For example, you might have an engine, an electrical system, and a mechanical linkage driving some actuator.

1.  You see the whole machine isn't doing what it's supposed to.

2.  You hear the engine, so that's working.

3.  You take off some covers so that you can see the solenoid trying to drive the actuator.

4.  You see the linkage is broken.  Or you see that the solenoid isn't moving, but if you move it by hand, the actuator moves.
This tells you whether it's a mechanical or an electrical problem.  You proceed from there.
The assumptions in this way of thinking don't trasfer very well to natual systems, because those aren't designed by people.
What it's like inhabiting the mind of a designer:  You're instinctively asking questions like:
"Why is this part here?  Why is it shaped like this?  What does it do, what is it for?"
"Oh, it's braced this way to withstand forces in this direction.  Oh, this part has to be precise so it's surface-ground, but that part doesn't so it's rough from the mold."

"Oh, this pivots like that to engage this other thing.  Oh, that cutout is so this part can fit through during assembly.  Oh, that tab is a stopper that hits here at the limits of the range.  If it wasn't there this other part could shear off or get crushed."

This guy gets it: you're actually inhabiting the thought process of the desiger, and if their mind was messy, it's unpleasant.
https://t.co/77BG67ezBd

This girl gets it.  Her version is a little different, but goes even deeper into the basic assumptions in some ways.
"Decide you are going to fix the printer."
"How do you *know* the printer is broken?"
https://t.co/N6LaoZRVsj
So again, the assumptions that this mindset encourages include:
"Everything that's here is here for a reason, even if that reason is just that it was easier to leave it in place than to cut it away."
"Everything is monocausal and either Broken or Fixed."
"If you restore it to the way it was originally, it will work the way it's supposed to."

"If you replace a broken part with a hacked/fabricated part that accomplishes the same thing, the whole machine will work basically the same again."  

"Only the business-end of a switch matters to the machine."

"Parts don't have agency or consciousness, but can have somewhat mysterious and delicate 'grooves' where they behave well, but fail if that groove is disturbed."
https://t.co/qI1C22JCIT

grrr I just spent almost an hour fighting with software to print twelve envelopes.  I could have hand-lettered them with calligraphy in that time.  And I'd be in a better mood.  

lesson there about the deceptive lure of power tools.
reality contains a surprising amount of detail: printing slog edition
first look up how to use an envelope template in LibreOffice.  a little janky but simple enough.
fill out the addresses, do the formatting, try to print.  size & orientation wrong.  selected #10 envelope, but it's still printing portrait, even though the preview window showed it correct.
search through every single menu for "Portrait / Landscape" selection.  Isn't there.  OK.  Have wasted about 5 envelopes at this point.

Export as PDF.  try this about 6 times before getting the size right.  Have to hunt to uncheck the "scale to fit" box at every menu.  Early versions margins are off for reasons unknown.  Fix by repositioning in original document.
Try printing in Preview, doesn't work.  Same orientation problem.
Try printing in Adobe Reader.  Works but margins are wrong.  Second try, crashes printer.  Printer dead.  Won't even turn on.
Try power cycling printer by unplugging at the printer.  Doesn't work.  Won't turn on.
Walk away, calm down.
Try unplugging the power adapter from the outlet.  Printer turns on again.  Progress.

Suspicious of Adobe Reader now, I try one more margin change and printing from Preview again.  For some reason "Export Directly as PDF" is different than "Export as PDF."  

The *Directly* version works.
Now that I have the margins good enough, the printer jams.  It can't feed a stack of envelopes the way it can paper.

Reality contains a surprising amount of detail: envelopes are actually four sheets thick, at their thickest.  In that one spot where the front, folded back, sides, and top all meet at the corner.  Printer frequently sucks in the whole stack.  Jams.

So after clearing the job, clearing the jams, I sit there, like a chump, holding the stack of envelopes between my thumb and forefinger, letting the top one off every time.

Defeats the point of the whole exercise, which was to walk away while it prints ~50.
Moving, accommodating, and setting up the power tool takes longer than doing the job with the hand tool.

Still thinking about encouraging people to post "Best Practices Docs", and one big hang-up seems to be, the people who most *should* post them are the same people who are most aware that their current workflow has room for improvement.

The youtuber Mr. Chickadee is a great example of this.  An amazing craftsman, his signature style was ZERO talking in videos.  No tell only show.  Finally in an interview one time he was asked about it, and said roughly:
"A lot of this stuff I was doing for the first time.  I didn't feel qualified to tell anyone else how to do it, or say that this was the correct way."
It's not an instruction, only a documentation.
ok, like half the value of the "Not Yet Best Practices" documentation is that it at least *identifies the problems* that the best practices need to solve.

I'm gonna suck it up here and turn this into a thread of my "Not Yet Best Practices" on photographing book pages, because they're fresh in my mind from the Bobos in Paradise section I just uploaded, and my process is a horrendous grind.  But the output is halfway decent.

Requirements:
1.  Good light
2.  Phone camera
3.  Flat surface
4.  Stiff board for holding the page flat to the surface.
5.  Image editing software

So the first problem is shadows from your hand/phone on the page.  You either gotta do this in front of a big window (even, natural light), or with two big overhead lamps that you can move around to eliminate the major shadows.  Or bounce light off the ceiling.

Next problem is that the binding doesn't lay flat.  My solution is to pin down the page I'm photographing with a Hardboard(HDF) clipboard using one hand.  Mine is brown, you know, that standard thin pressboards stuff.  A white one would be better for reflecting even light.

This is a delicate, finicky, and tricky thing, depending on the thickness of the book, the stiffness of the binding, how close to the beginning or end you are, etc.  It collapses if you don't keep the pressure just right.

One hand is tied up holding the board, so you only have one hand for the camera.  Gotta hold that steady.  And also compose it as close to "flat" as you can.  Get your rectangles as near square as you can.

Then you upload the photos.  This is where it turns into a massive grind.  My version system is horrible.  You end up with 3 or 4 image files for each page.  The raw image, the cropped image, the processed image, and then the processed imaged saved as a small file for web.
gah, I don't want to think about this anymore, it's too horrible.  maybe to be continued ...

This thread got me thinking the other day, two opposing social pressures:
1.  Make the work look hard so my achievement is impressive.
2. Make the work look easy to my talent is impressive.
https://t.co/FBcreQOT9i

And you would think that the two effects might cancel each other out somehow.  

But no.
Instead, they represent a kind of pure mental drag along a third dimension.  The more you care and calculate about how you're perceived, the less attention you can pay to the work itself.

The work suffers, of course, but that's not even what most people notice.  What they notice is the emotional tangle, the conflict.  The bragging and downplaying leak out in small, idiosyncratic ways.  They leave a signature of doubt.  Insecurity.  Turmoil.

Today seems like a good day to talk about a sort of odd discipline I practice, which is writing various types of paperwork by hand.  Invoices, receipts, time sheets, quotes, etc.

I have this idea that to understand what one of these documents *is,* you ought to be able to recall the important fields from scratch.  I mean, most of the time it's pretty basic.  Names, dates, what is being transacted, where, some contact info.

"any expedition plan should fit on the back of a napkin"
"and business plan should fit on the back of a napkin"
"any contract should fit on the back of a napkin"
You're practicing for writing those types of plans and contracts.  Name the parties, name the goods, number the sums, sign.

One way to think of it is like writing a letter.  It's like a reality check on your common-sense ability to understand the transaction.  You want to make it readable enough that if someone has to come in later and digitize the damn thing, they'll see readily what happened.

I find that doing this sometimes offers an almost psychedelic insight into the nature of how authority is minted.  Can be spooky, but also, if you're doing it right, and you're being honest and straight-up, it feels great.

Computers down?  Printers down?

"Where we're going, we don't need forms!"
there is a dark side too, where you can glimpse how easy it would be to use paperwork as a bullying tactic, and of course that's everywhere.
there's a fun element of historical LARPing, if you've ever looked at important old documents and seen how simple they can be

the trippy part is kind of seeing how "precision is an aesthetic," and the same information can be carried by less precise means.  But the aesthetic can *really* change how people perceive legitimacy.
there's also something to be said about narrowing the world to fit the templates you're given, vs starting from a blank slate and deciding afterwards what information is important.

an additive vs exclusionary approach to paperwork

like a sculptor adding material with clay vs carving away stone
idk if this is making sense to anyone

@hermittoday In my limited experience, doing things *for* other people rarely works, and can backfire hard.  In Marie Kondo's TV show, she doesn't really do much, she seems to serve as an ideal persona that the clients try to live up to.
@hermittoday She's a funny combination of emulable fantasy (WWMKD?) and authority figure (Oh no, better get all the non-sparking-joy clutter out of sight before the boss shows up!)

@hermittoday It's very tricky.  I view MK's success as evidence of how enormous the need for this service is, but putting it into practice is very difficult.  I'll take this as a prompt to start listing off pitfalls.
@hermittoday 1.  people don't really notice the change, even if it does make them feel better, it does so on a subconscious level, and they don't maintain it.
@hermittoday 2.  people resist change if they don't see it as coming from themselves, or worse, going against some inner logic that you, the interloper, can't see.
@hermittoday 3.  two people can end up settling into a master and maidservant dynamic: one person messes, the other cleans, and nothing ever changes

@hermittoday 4. you succeed in getting someone to see the "affordance," and they realize that the move is possible, but don't assign any weight or permanence to the change they've made

"I put the table here.  I can put the table ANYWHERE!"  And then the table becomes another piece of clutter

@hermittoday 5.  people stay fixated on *using the environment* to perform their task.  Taking the environment for granted, not really perceiving it as a thing itself, a thing that can be changed.  They immediately adopt the change, without noticing it.

@hermittoday you’re trying to teach someone to do a very familiar action that they already know how to do, literally just picking things up and putting them down, but in a new way where they’re assigning a new significance to it.

@hermittoday I’d never quite noticed the parallels to Alexander Technique before.  It all sounds very woo.

@hermittoday I will say that although it may not be possible to learn from a book, I tend to see people *reading* those kinds of books as an EXTREMELY good sign on its own.  It means the desire and awareness is there, and it's just looking for a model to emulate.

@hermittoday Imitation is actually one of the best ways to teach this that avoids the big pitfall in #2.

The "moral obligation" is to do this in a space where others visit, in a way that makes them think "Hey, I could do a version of that in *my* house."

yoooo, here’s an awesome parallel between Larry McEnerney’s second analysis of the Gettysburg Address and this video essay by screenwriter Michael Arndt about great endings:
The screenwriting point is: an awesome ending comes when the climax snatches victory from the jaws of defeat in *three different* sets of stakes.

1.  External stakes (do they get the carrot, or the stick?)
2.  Emotional stakes (were they right to trust their gut?)
3.  Philosophical stakes (is their worldview validated, when it seemed detractors were about to be proven right?)
At Gettysburg, Lincoln does the same thing.  The audience has assembled there to grieve.  He tells them: you actually have *three problems.*

1.  We might lose the civil war.  (practical stakes)
2.  A bunch of people have just died, and we’re here to mourn them (emotional stakes)
3.  This war is testing whether *any* Democracy can long endure (philosophical stakes)

Taking these honored dead as motivation to renew the audience’s devotion to the same cause they died for, Lincoln is arguing that that’s the only way to solve ANY of those problems.  Mourning will be meaningless if those dead have died in vain.

"inherently non-viral"

To go all Pirsig for a minute, *quality* is both inherently non-viral, and inherently viral. https://t.co/AWwuKqV5id
Inherently viral in the sense that people can sense it, and naturally copy it wherever it exists.
Inherently non-viral in the sense that *copying* cannot *create* quality, therefore it always decays over time as the context drifts, bc quality *is* suitability to context.  Fitment.
I guess the lesson here is that whenever you copy anything, you should assume by default that it will need modification to your particular circumstance.

@forthrighter Shigo's among the highest authorities anyone might appeal to on this topic, and he would call everything we've been discussing so far "topping." https://t.co/yVzG8kfWD9

@forthrighter According to Shigo, and probably most Master Gardeners in Europe and Japan, true pollarding must be trained from a young age, to establish the form, and then keep it that way, cutting back the suckers to the same "ball" every time. https://t.co/fSqjVh2BFe

@forthrighter So really, we're talking about two separate things:

1.  Pollarding, which is not great for tree health, but has a rich heritage of its own, sort of like bonsai.

@forthrighter 2.  Topping a venerable (very large and highly compromised) old tree to a much smaller size (roughly 8'-30' tall) and hoping it might survive, and eventually come to *resemble* a tree that had been pollarded properly to begin with.

@forthrighter AFAIK there is no widely agreed upon term for #2, so most people, even arborists, still call it "pollarding."  Hence my confusion.  

The best name I can think of is "False Pollarding" but that doesn't seem too likely to catch on.

@forthrighter my understanding (from the Blair Glenn vid above) is that the practice originated as a source for straighter wood than you would otherwise reliably get.  If you let the suckers grow for ... say 5 years or more, you get some pretty straight posts.
@forthrighter Harvesting more frequently, you get flexible sticks for woven fencing, or yearly, shoots to weave into baskets, especially with willow.

@forthrighter So it came from practical necessity.  A further tree-biology factor to keep in mind is that the tree maintains its root system, so all the new growth happening above ground, it doesn't need to grow new roots.  So it's more efficient at producing building & fire wood.

I think the error is in the word "destroying," which assumes that accountability exists by default, or used to exist in better forms.
When new capacities sprout, accountability is absent, by definition.

You can think about it from a childhood-development lens.

The capacity to do harm precedes the capacity to understand harm, precedes the capacity to refrain from doing harm.

"Stop hitting your sister."

The new civilizational capacities that seem to be destroying everything lack accountability by default, because they are new.  They aren't even institutional capacities really, they are emergent capacities.
This is not to say that many people at the helm of industry aren't taking advantage of the ambiguity here, and shunting responsibility in unethical ways.
It's merely to say that you have to try shit without fully understanding the consequences, even under the best of circumstances.

You have to fuck around, before you can find out.

Modernity is the ability to fuck around with previously unfathomable amounts of energy (petroleum, nuclear) & precision.

so instead of the frame: "Oh no!  How can we stop accountability from eroding?"

we need the frame: "How does accountability grow in the wild?"

Vannevar Bush  "Science - The Endless Frontier"

I often rag on recycling but I’m not against recycling per se.  I am frustrated at recycling as an instance of a larger pattern of complexification & confusion affecting regular people making decisions in a consumer society.
You want to get rid of something.  You try to get rid of something.  And your mind won’t let you.  Your culture won't let you.
There is a series of hurdles.

“I might use the thing.”  - keep it
“Somebody might want the thing” - sell it
“Somebody might use the thing” - donate
“It might be recycleable” -  …  what?  Figure out whether it is recycleable?  Nobody knows.
“Nobody wants the thing.” - OK, fine. Finally, we can throw it away.
You never get to throw things away.  The decision gets shunted until eventually a less conscientious person is put in charge.

Often I think being a good mechanic is about emotional management, and here is a long and ongoing story to illustrate.  Time and again:
>"Ah hah!  This must be the problem!"
>Trials and tribulations to fix that problem.
>That problem was not the underlying problem.

OK, the problem is: main climbing chainsaw, a junky old husqvarna but it's what I got.  Chain brake stops working, brake-band snapped.  OK, simple enough, order the part.  Part arrives in a couple weeks.  Swapping it out should be simple enough, right? https://t.co/sUlEa34BhT

I'm gonna have to dive into some finnicky innards that I'm not familiar with.  There will be springs under tension, and fiddly little linkages.  I'm a bit nervous but just start taking covers off.  Just do the obvious thing.  Expose & clean, understanding may come. https://t.co/0kRAaSPy7q

For some reason I am much more comfortable removing screws and bolts than springs and clips.  Things could fly and get lost.  Things might be held in tension you don't understand.  Since it's an unfamiliar system, you won't remember how it goes back together.

I'm really hoping that I can replace that band without taking the spring & linkage apart.  I even manage to twist the old one out, removing an anchor screw at an awful angle.  Alas, there's no way the new one's going back in that way.  At this point, I turn to YouTube. https://t.co/RBdBYCkUYc

I can't find the same model saw, but there's a bunch of guys doing this repair on similar husq saws, and one who seems sensible enough just taps the linkage pin out of the magnesium clutch cover body.  Only partway out, not all the way.

Since I have a super tiny punch handy, that works well enough, and goes easier than expected.  Something about working on press-fit parts just makes me nervous.  Will they ever fit properly tightly again?  Isn't this a factory operation? https://t.co/mPQNlr96q2

But once the linkage is free, everything is so much easier.  Obviously this is the correct way to do this.  It would be absolutely impossible to wiggle the part in otherwise.  The moment of truth: reassembly & tapping the pin back flush.  It goes fine.  Snap rings too. https://t.co/w7PkQCL59u

Feeling satisfied, & having expanded my mechanical confidence a bit with the pin removal & re-installation process, I put the saw back together, figuring the test will be a mere afterthought.

No.  Saw won't run.  Engine bogs.  Chain will barely spin.

I've been at this for an hour or two.  It's not the time, so much as mentally the time *operating in unfamiliar territory.*  Made a little more dangerous by knowing failure means spending half a grand on a new saw.  Which I probably should have done ... years ago.
Anyway, I call it a day.  I definitely need to return to this problem fresh.

So today, I return fresh.  Maybe it's a simple thing.  Maybe it's a dumb mistake.  Maybe it just needs to be broken in.  Maybe lower chain tension would help?  

You're hoping there's something obvious you missed last time ... which is ... kind of a sour hope.
No.  I remove & reattach the clutch cover a few times, cleaning & inspecting the chain & bar.  I don't know what I'm looking for.

At this point I'm starting to suspect:
"The saw only worked before because the brake-band was worn so thin.  Even if there's nothing wrong with the new part, and it's installed correctly, this is not going to work."

Everything spins freely with the clutch cover off.  No problem with the engine.  

At one point I think I may have been misaligning the cover the whole time, pinching one corner.

>"Oh, it seemed like the nut wasn't going far enough down on those threads ...?"

No.

It seems like the band is getting pinched between the cover and the drum in these two spots, creating major drag on the drum all the time.  I keep looking for ways the band might not be sitting in the channel the way it's supposed to.  But no, it's right. https://t.co/DXYr6JDUFL

At this point I try the desperate measure of filing down the soft magnesium "stops" or guides circled above.  Obviously, this isn't how it's supposed to be done, but maybe a little relief there will be enough to get the saw functional?

Every time I test the saw the band and the drum is super  hot to the touch.  I'm probably burning up the clutch.  Could the drum be out of shape?  Ovalized or ... expanded somehow?  Maybe from heat during use?  I examine it closely, with calipers, for eccentricity.  No.

At this point I start to test various versions of "Will the saw run with less drag?"

>crazy loose chain?
>tiny improvement, but no.

Next I try without bar & chain.  I start peering in the underside during the tests.  

Oh, with no bar to clamp things are skewed, adding a second set of potential drags.

OK, try "Bar only, but no chain."  That'll be a good pure test of the drag the brake band is putting on the engine.

Still lots of drag.  OK.  This is progress.  Now I have a new potential culprit: the drive-sprocket-clutch drum.
@Hispeedlowdrug They look & feel okay, & they couldn't possibly have become *too strong* could they?   I'm gettin there ... see what you can spot: https://t.co/8YNqK5Gukh
And better yet, when I take it apart after that test, a NEW CLUE!  There is fresh wear on the magnesium clutch cover!  That wasn't there before! https://t.co/NoZVJ7LEsu

And there's matching wear on the exposed clutch parts.  Further investigation shows play in the drum/sprocket, sliding on the drive shaft.  There should probably be a bit, but that much?? https://t.co/spUpw8DXlI

Invigorated by my new hypothesis, I gear up to take apart the clutch.  AGAIN an intimidating operation I've never done before, or maybe done once or twice in the distant past, & on different models.  I consult youtube for a refresher.

Anchoring the driveshaft involves jamming something relatively soft, but also stiff, in through the spark plug hole to act as a stop for the piston.  String recommended, try one kind, it's too thin, find thicker, that works.  At least the clutch has a nice solid 3/4" hex to grab. https://t.co/CcD0jNNVmh

After a couple tries of "string not thick enough" & thanks to youtube university's Reverse Threading tip, which I otherwise would have to scour some manual for, the clutch comes off without difficulty.
However, new problem:  There Is No Problem!

Everything looks fine?  The needle bearing isn't shot?  It's not any better when I put it back together?  

I mean, I could always just start replacing parts, and the sprocket/drum is the obvious candidate, along with maybe the needle bearing ...
But I'm tired of throwing good money after bad on this saw, & I'm not gonna do that if I don't understand the problem.  At this point getting the diagnosis is a matter of principle.

At this point, we've arrived at the essential and under-discussed-in-repair-manuals:

"sitting around looking at it time™"

Now that the drum is off I can put it inside the brake band and see how much room there is.  There's tons, plenty.

I keep thinking about how the problem all seems to be on this side.  That's where the old band wore out.  The new band already has wear marks there. https://t.co/hFc4gRIxKD

"The band's not too tight, it's off center!"  I say to myself.  But what part is off?  What's the reference center?  The whole clutch cover is anchored in place by non-adjustable pins that could not possibly have moved or worn that much ... right?

Looking again, and looking at the driveshaft which was previously concealed by the clutch ("What a waste of time taking the whole clutch off, it wasn't even the problem!") I spot a small but curious eccentricity.  Could this be the thing? https://t.co/U4WTOloKNW

Look at the gap on the left.  Look at the worn housing/casting ring on the right.  The whole driveshaft has ... moved?  Drifted?  There's no play in it, it's not a bearings problem.  What is going on here? https://t.co/IHvbHxnBwV

And that's where it stands now.  I'm looking at schematics trying to figure out ... how this whole ... I think you'd call it a Crankcase ... could have worn or moved like that?

Chief Chainsaw Officer nailed it, of course.  Good eye.
https://t.co/gTOjC7gLVr

Is that a bad metaphor?  Maybe.

Our current system of rules grew out of a simpler one.  Does it ever go the other way?

We didn't design the current system from scratch.  Every rule was made to deter a then-current "bad thing."  

When bad thing is gone, the rule remains.

Currently seeing this as "Every rule is an overreaction to a then-current invasive phenomenon."

You kinda gotta watch invasive species sweep through and then chill out over ~20 years to begin to not be freaked out by them as a class.
Every rule is a loss of trust in our ability to handle things on a case-by-case basis?
The *existence* of a rule for everything *atrophies* our ability to handle things on a case-by-case basis.
Are rules always a ratcheting phenomenon?
The unreasonable effectiveness of starting from scratch.

The Texas Sunset Commission is one of the only optimistic things I've heard of on this front.  Are there any other entities like it?
Human bones are renewed by osteoclast and osteoblast cells, operating in balance with each other.  Clasts devour old bone tissue, Blasts re-make.
Are there analogous entities in governance?  Are we in "grow-only governance mode" because we're still in a growth economy?
Even that's probably a bad metaphor, because there are two opposite principles at work.  Oldest rules are most lindy, OR oldest rules are most obsolete.
I guess the way to differentiate is to ask whether the rule was written in response to a transient phenomenon.

I basically accept the Lasch/TLP premise that modern American culture is becoming particularly narcissistic, driven in part by media trends.  And because it's more ubiquitous, it's harder to spot.
But I've never heard the underlying psychology spelled out so clearly.  Nancy McWilliams is obviously a genius.  Who is she?

Turns out she's an old school psychoanalyst.  Psychoanalysis is sort of out of fashion these days, cause it's slow, expensive, and has been largely supplanted by psychiatry, CBT, and various other modalities.

A lot of psychologists grumble about the absurdity of the DSM, but she actually came up with an alternative and wrote a whole giant textbook about it.  It didn't succeed at toppling DSM supremacy, but it made a dent.
https://t.co/f7aF8HvmzU

Here she is twelve years ago, in more direct "Confronting the DSM" mode.  She wasn't as great a public speaker back then, but I'm AMAZED at how clearly she is able to describe a subtle and pernicious paradigm change in her field.
https://t.co/rpP7N9Vm02
“This conflation of what is good for research with what is good for treatment has contributed to a subtle but to my mind sinister paradigm shift. ..."

"Psychotherapy used to be generally understood as a healing relationship.  …  Recently, it seems to have been re-conceived as a set of techniques to be applied to certain specifiable, discrete types of suffering.”

“Therapists have historically seen ourselves as practitioners of an art … but we are increasingly pressured to define ourselves as technicians in the service of the narrowest possible definition of symptomatic improvement.”

Ok, end quote.  One consequence of DSM’s prominence is that when young therapists are trained in it, they naturally think in terms of it.  So “Mental Health” becomes defined by *absence,* not by presence.  The absence of symptoms, rather than the presence of health.

So in line with “focus on what you want to see more of,” here she is talking about what the positive criteria she’s come up with for psychological vitality and well-being.
https://t.co/Zlyfycxn2z

That talk is also where I came across the general principle behind this tweet, which people seemed to appreciate:
https://t.co/B8pVDbsAS0
(13:37 if you want to look for it.)  It's an class of things I've so-far been calling "false spectrums" although that name isn't quite right.

The idea is: there's a spectrum with two poles.  And you might expect "good" to be a moderate position on the spectrum.

But no.  In fact "good" means to be capable and comfortable inhabiting either pole.  (And not prone to getting stuck there either.)
Anyway, in a culture obsessed with finding "secret third things" I thought you guys might appreciate this pattern.

OK the last thing I'll point out in this big "Nancy McWilliams is great" thread is that she has *perfect* expert's tone.  You really notice it in the very first video in the thread, because that's her in a series of other therapists talking about the same topic.

It's really about how an expert speaker relates to jargon.  It's about what not to do.

1.  You don't use jargon to bully.
2.  You don't *retreat* to jargon when challenged.
3.  You don't see it as your job to *defend* the jargon.
You can see this in the way she explains the things in layman's terms first, and only THEN drops the jargon as a footnote, almost an afterthought.
If she led with the jargon, your mind would be hard at work trying to pattern-match the definition that comes afterwards.  So you'd be less able to actually listen.

What are some ways that technology could make your life more difficult, in a satisfying way?
The basic problem with technology is that it optimizes for *convenience*, which people think they want but doesn't actually make them happy.
And the ways our technological environments make our lives more annoying now are all about complexity and uncertainty.
"This technology will make your life more convenient, except when it breaks and then it's very UNSATISFYINGLY difficult & tricky to get it working again."
Our lives are more difficult now because there are more manuals to read and more settings to learn and more updates to install, and none of those are satisfying maintenance tasks.

What's a satisfying maintenance task?  Oiling your leather shoes, maybe.  Sharpening things, maybe?

Sure, it's a mindset thing maybe, but nobody's ever going to find fixing a workflow broken by a software update satisfying.

Pondering a cryptic assertion:

"The problem with commodification is not a moral problem."
what gets measured gets managed --> what gets measured gets destroyed
measurement is the first step to optimization, optimization is the first step to commodification, commodification is the route to quality going down.
Commodification means putting a floor on quantity, without concern for where the ceiling is.
Commodification means choosing NOT to measure quality within certain bounds.  Only quantity.
So once you've successfully commodified something, you build the *habit* of not looking at quality
... which, according to the preference for maximizing the measured variable, eventually degrades.
Abstraction, scaling, leverage.  To commodify something is to operate at a scale where you can't afford to examine the material itself.  You have chosen to spend your attention elsewhere.
Someone dealing in commodities is dealing in abstractions, and has forfeited the ability to care about the precise nature of the goods being transacted.

Thinking about a passage that stuck with me, that I can’t find.  It was a magazine profile or book review of Jordan Peterson, back when he was first emerging as a popular figure.
It wasn’t a hit piece, but it wasn’t exactly a puff piece either.  The author seemed puzzled.
The writer was struggling to understand Peterson’s popularity, given that his advice sounded basically trite and common sense.
And the answer he eventually proposed was that Peterson was a prophet.  Maybe he used a different word?  But the gist as I remember it was:
“Prophets only ever say the same few things:  ‘God is real, Evil is real, you shouldn’t do it.  Redemption is possible.’"

"But they say it with such conviction and fire in their eyes that everywhere they go people seem to respond  
‘Uh oh, guess he’s a prophet.  Better drop to our knees and repent.’”
Anyway, right now this is striking me as another case of cleverness voice.  The reviewer is clever.  And cleverness is marveling at the very possibility that it might be irrelevant.
“He’s not saying anything clever.  So why is it working?  Why the hell are all these people listening?!?”

The way disruptive technologies cause intergenerational strife is by destroying coherent ethics of thrift.
The old generation is set in their ways, the young generation screams "______ is too cheap to meter!"
Recorded media made frozen genius too cheap to meter.
Anyone can own *the best* recordings.
You can be surrounded by as much genius as you can withstand.
And what this did, is it made *curation* seem like the most pressing problem of cultural triage.
If you were a culture worker, it was more important to curate and preserve and promote your own personal library of the *best* recordings, than it was to try and exceed them yourself.
Curating frozen genuis took priority over practicing real-time intelligence.

Vibes on the TL seemed particularly rank yesterday.  (hence the return of wildlife posting.)  Got me thinking about emotional dynamics on social media ...

In small groups our emotions can *accomplish* something.  They can signal a truth to the group.  

"This issue *matters* to that person."
But on the internet our emotions don't accomplish that, because without stable connections, emotions only feed psychofauna.

The lack of choice in the real group makes you take the emotion seriously.  On the internet there's an endless supply of "better" people who're *not* grumpy.  Who are focusing on what they want to see more of.
I'm wondering to myself how this differs from so-called "toxic positivity"?

On the internet, or above dunbar-scale, people filter bubble via emotional tone.

"I don't want [_____ form of unpleasantness], so I practice good algorithm hygiene by pruning it early and often."
Okay, that is sensible, but you've just lost the signal from smart people who are occasionally angry for good reason.

The flip side of this is that, from the side of the emotion-haver, your emotions *feel* impotent.  You can tell that they're not working.

I suspect this has something to do with the rise of the word "cucked" to common parlance.
I don't have a solution.  That's how cybermedieval works.  Crowds are dumb.  Whaddaya gonna do?

Well, you're gonna do the same thing every sensible person is already doing: isolating further and focusing only on what they can control.

Abandoning the public sphere.  *Brain Draining* the public sphere.
What's the public sphere gonna look like in ten, twenty years, once it's been laying fallow (or being battle-ravaged by bots and madmen) the whole time?
