This is what material literacy looks like. https://t.co/hzuXCTZ2ok

Just an awareness that materials have properties.  A lot of properties, more than you could ever count.  But knowing a handful of relatively basic and intuitive ones is enough to start making educated guesses.
Whenever two different materials are next to each other ... weird stuff might happen.  Because they have different properties.

-expansion/contraction from drying & wetting
-expansion/contraction from heating & cooling
-tension and compression
-flexibility vs brittleness
-strength in one axis but not in another
-adhesion vs ... what you might call "internal integrity" of the paint
You don't need to name all these properties, or learn the engineering terms, although it helps.  You just need to start going through life noticing that every material is different.
Stone from wood from metal from rubber from paint from tar from paper from plastic from clay from brick from cotton from wax.  And each of those has its own huge spectrum.

You end up becoming a student of patina, of weathering and aging and mechanical failure.  Why did that form?  Why did that separate?  Why did it separate *there* but not anywhere else?  What made that surface?  Why that color?

Narcissism is a particular pattern of stuckness that tends to arise as a response to being in a hungry ghost realm.

Some components:
-Not having enough
-Need to get more for yourself
-Convincing yourself that you *deserve* more than you have

-Contempt and disdain as distancing tactics—from people who have less
-Envy and resentment towards people who have more.
And finally, a reflexive *de-valuing* of what you already have.  This reflexive de-valuing serves two functions:

1)  It allows you to maintain hope: "I will feel better once I get ________"
2)  It explains your current state:  "I'm miserable/unsatisfied, must be because what I already have is bad/trivial/inadequate."
In the consumer society this may seem to be about objects and products and money.  For a creative it will be about respect and recognition.  Probably it all traces back to status and acceptance.
Now the really crazy thing is that America is an entire *nation* caught in the grips of this pattern.
Do you remember how the original premise of neoliberalism was that comparative advantage dictates China become the world's manufacturer and the US be the world's consumer?

Our entire entertainment-advertising industrial complex is geared towards nudging us into this state of dissatisfied, acquisitive narcissism, and reassuring us that this is a normal way to be.  It is not.

@natural_hazard I have a book on the history of industrial design called "Objects of Desire" that goes into the biz/employment structure of furniture & clothing around this time.  Sounded ... dismal.  Thin margins, many races to the bottom.

@natural_hazard There does seem like a sense in which having, say, 3 tiers of workers (weavers, patternmakers, seamstresses, detail-sewers for buttons & lace) will be working at cross purposes when each is trying to optimize their quantity-per-hour, cause that's what they're paid by.

@natural_hazard And the central premise of Taylorism is you can slash that infighting for efficiency gainz if there's ONE agent overseeing the whole system tweaking here and there, finding the bottlenecks, and paying everyone hourly instead.

@natural_hazard You can see the logic of Taylor not wanting any worker to try to "improve" his particular part of the process, because that just makes his parts non-interchageable with the other shift's, and interchangable parts are what started this whole industrial engine.

@natural_hazard So Western Taylorism was brutally hierarchal, but the Japanese translated Taylor and a ton of other factory technical manuals almost immediately after they were written, and seem to have allowed much more leeway for production line workers to offer feedback on processes.

@LoschbourStev This question nerdsniped me because I've encountered some esoteric stuff about harvesting wood on a new moon in midwinter for maximum rot resistance.
@LoschbourStev ... iirc the research came from some people who tried to repair stave churches, and had their new wood rot within a decade or so, when the original wood was ... at least centuries old.

@LoschbourStev The theory, sensibly enough and matching what I know of fruit trees, is that cold-climate trees generally:
1. go dormant in winter and store their energy (sugars) in their roots.  that's why fruit trees are pruned in winter, it minimizes the energy you take from them.

@LoschbourStev 2. are really, really, more than you might expect, sensitive to light.  So not only do they "notice" daily and yearly cycles, they even notice moon cycles.  And put less fluid up to the leaves/needles during the new moon.

@LoschbourStev So that's the theory: you harvest in the depths of winter, at the new moon, to have the least sugar in the wood.  Less sugar --> less calories for bugs or fungus to feast on --> long-lasting rot-resistant wood.

@LoschbourStev This ALSO matches certain lore I've heard from extreme firewood snobs, about how summer-cut wood never fully dries and will always be smoky/crackly, and only winter-cut wood is acceptable.
@LoschbourStev Anyway that was my reconstructed memory of this theory, never having really tested any of it, but ... obviously evergreens DO continue to photosynthesize in winter, at least somewhat.
@LoschbourStev So ... how did the stave churches get that extreme level of rot resistance?  Pretty sure Norway doesn't have massive oak or maple forests, they're mostly evergreens up there right?

@LoschbourStev Aside here to note the other two methods I know of for making rot-resistant wood: you soak it in water until it becomes "driftwood" (again, all the sugars leach out) or you char the outside (half an inch or so) which at least creates a barrier that prevents damage from entering.
@LoschbourStev OK, here is where we get into the truly wierd stuff that was so confusing I sort of blocked it out when I read it the first time.
@LoschbourStev It also, like bonsai and pollarding, could be construed as tree-torture, and therefore offends modern arborist sensibilities.

@LoschbourStev The jumping off point for this was a cache of obscure PDFs linked below this video.  Video's still up, but the PDFs are linkrot.
https://t.co/Y1o4XPQmn6

@LoschbourStev They include three or four (?) unpublished and ... sketchily translated manuscripts from, well, some are Nordic, some are Swiss/Austrian.  Part of my confusion is that they seem to be describing almost opposite strategies.

@LoschbourStev One is the sort of "dessication strategy" that I describe above.  My guess is that "ringbarking" involves killing an evergreen tree by girdling it, letting it stand for quite some time while the fluid drains out via gravity, then felling.

@LoschbourStev The other strategy aims to create "fatwood" which is extremely FULL of so called "resin."  It is not clear to me how or if this resin is different from the sap normally present in living trees.

@LoschbourStev Nor is it clear to me why a high-enough concentration of resin would prevent, rather than encourage rot?  Maybe this resin is a special substance trees send to injured areas to prevent bug ingress, like white blood cells?

@LoschbourStev some claims I see about "Fatwood"
-the high concentrations of resin make it very hard
-it is superior for fine detailed carving
-it is good for small pieces like window frames
-it cannot be glued so is not condusive to modern factory woodworking techniques

@LoschbourStev My guess is that by various varieties of injuring the tree (spot-barking, strip-barking, and/or topping), you encourage the tree to send resin to the wound.  However there is also stuff about trying to *prevent* the tree from weeping sap, "keep the resin inside" so idk there.

@LoschbourStev There seem to be detailed schedules for doing this that I can't make much sense of.  One thing is clear, they are doing this with a long timeframe, the tree may be barked repeatedly over 20 years before being harvested.

@LoschbourStev Some other effects that may be at work:
-by hindering the tree, they are trying to slow growth, so as to get tighter growth rings.

@LoschbourStev -by strip-barking the tree, they are "pre-aging" the part that will remain in a rectangular cabin log?  or vise versa?  whichever side of the log is more rot-prone becomes the side that is hewn away in prep for construction. https://t.co/t9ECWBmm09

@LoschbourStev For now, that about concludes my latest ... pass over my own ignorance in this area.  Thanks for the original question, because it made me notice:  "Hey, the reason you thought that thing worked doesn't make sense at all."

@LoschbourStev Oh and one more thing: NONE of this explains at all why rainforest/equatorial wood is generally the most rot-resistant wood there is.  So there's obviously a whole other set of elements at work there.

@LoschbourStev I will note that cypress kinda "feels" like fatwood, in terms of oiliness, so maybe fatwood is a way for extreme northerners to mimic that?  Cypress is one of the rare soft/lightweight yet also rot resistant woods.  Other rot-resistant rainforest wood tends to be dense.

@LoschbourStev That matches my experience, dead standing wood tends to seem dry, and you'd think it'd be dry from standing up in the wind & sun for years, but then you measure it and it's ... yeah, 18, 20%.
@LoschbourStev What killed the all the oaks?

Downsizing fast enough that all your shit isn't janky and broken all the time is the core upper-class skill for the 21st century.
We're all downwardly mobile.  By a lot.  The trick is realizing it, accepting it, and getting ahead of it.
Even the billionaires are downwardly mobile because they don't have a cohesive or capable public to preside over.  They're de-skilling their own public as fast as they can.
Being upper class, in a material sense, is not about luxury items, or opulent items, or conspicuous consumption, or wealth signaling.
It's about your shit just working, all the time, every time, so that you can focus on your core competency, which is etiquette (or treachery, as the case may be.)
There's gonna be a bunch of janky ass sailboat guys, and schoolbus guys, and homesteading guys.  And by guys I mean families.
And they will be strong, and tough, and ingenious, and good people.  But their unconscious choice to be overextended and janky will consign them to the lower class.
Clinging to the idea of a 20th century lifestyle is how you lose this game.

Personally, I hate commissions.  Earned one once, it didn’t feel good.
I’m pretty much fine with hourly rates and day rates.
I hate giving quotes because you can’t charge for the quote, and the quote wrecks a day that would otherwise be free.
Basically I hate driving around, I hate extra “touch points” with the client, and I hate thinking about the problem more than once.
I show up at the job with my tools and no plan, and it’s done once it’s done, then I hand you the bill.
I also hate all the mental gymnastics—you end up doing all this mental math of whether your quote was a triumph or a loss.  I don't wanna waste my brain on that!
Bonuses … meh, fine I guess.  If the biz has a good year why not spread it around?  Problematic if they become expected.
Stock market investing: fine I guess.  I mean, probably morally bankrupt but it’s what you gotta do to be part of the modern world unless you go full goldbug.
Short term trading: eh, feels sleazy.  Wins don’t feel real … or … deserved.
I’m fine with retail markups.  No problem with em.  You’re paying for convenience, they gotta pay the rent on bricks & mortar.
Royalties?  A man can dream ...
Now patents, on the other hand ... don't get me started.  Patents are pure evil.

In the interest of promoting repair and material literacy, I thought I'd share my "indoor/delicate repair & sewing kit."  I'd be curous to see yours. https://t.co/Gt0Kl99cLu
The tiny tubes of Shoe Goo have been a game changer!  Found them at a big box sewing store.  Funny how the male-coded big box stores only have big tubes that inevitably dry up, leaving you stranded.
@DefenderOfBasic That is exactly the dream!  I come visit for 3-10 days or so, fix what I can, & help you set up your own repair kits.

Here's a little experimental repair that worked out for a super annoying problem.  And all it took was cutting extra material off.

Original Failure Mode: broken spray bottle trigger on some windex. https://t.co/YvhJXOL9oq

Design Criticism: the spray bottle *sprays better* the harder you squeeze it!  The mist gets better and better right up until the point that the whole mechanism catastrophically fails, leaving you stranded.  How is the hapless ham-fisted user to know?

But I found another spray bottle head for a different cleaning bottle that was empty.

Problem: donor bottle had this "ratcheting tightening mechanism" to keep it from coming off, while the windex bottle had this lip preventing the replacement cap from screwing down. https://t.co/9ytK2nFQtF

What needs to happen: just remove material from the bottom of the cap.  But how?  It's plastic!  You could maybe put it down on a belt sander, but that would gum up your sander, and not clear how to get the tube out first.
So I left the whole mess on my kitchen table for a while, pondering the situation.  "What I really need is a hot knife.  But I don't have one of those, and I don't even know what a good one is."

Then I remembered the little "thread sealer" in my sewing kit.  It's a tiny little pen thing, only powered by a single AA batery, intended only to cut threads.  But why not give it a try? https://t.co/OiZSGvyisd

To my surprise it worked!  Just plunge straight in and go around in a circle.  It was slow going, maybe 1mm/s, so the whole circumference took, maybe a minute or so, which feels painfully slow if you're accustomed to power tools.  But it worked!  Stood outside in the wind. https://t.co/yR6cLEvLdg
After that I heated the tip again and wiped off the melted plastic with a cotton rag, which also straightened it out (impatience and pushing too hard had bent the tip over.) https://t.co/slU4xHkQVt

Also to do a bit of design appreciation in addition to design criticism: smart how the cap securely covers the button, making sure it can't be pressed when it's bouncing around in your kit.  Good design. https://t.co/NiS1Wz1Fu6

Open questions: is there some sort of technical language for the thread patterns on spray bottles?  How would you figure that out?  And where can you buy a replacement trigger?  That's the "Technical Purchasing" way of dealing with the problem.

Good design is invisible.  So invisible, in fact, that most people can’t see it at first, and refuse to acknowledge it’s importance, or even existence.

some can’t see it because it’s boring
some can’t see it because it’s subtle
some can’t see it because it’s technical
most can’t see it if it doesn’t affect their lives in an immediate and direct way
only by dramatic tales of momentous loss and unfathomable waste are people concerned with power convinced to pay attention to minor details like design.
“Just works” is the ultimate design criteria, but if you’re a power-oriented person, and your job, your milieu, your whole life is focused on climbing a power hierarchy, then guess what ...?
Everything already appears to “just work” because everything is done for you by distant servants that you barely ever interact with, and certainly never *hear from.*
Anything that doesn’t “just work” is structurally hidden from you because any interaction with a tool or system that doesn’t “just work” is instantly delegated to some poor sucker.

Been contemplating the tension between:  "Giving people stuff does not elevate them morally or spiritually."
vs.  
"Poverty & desperation clearly can degrade a person morally & spiritually."
Seems like a tragic condition, a no-win situation.  Lose or break-even.

It's also where I think the US is now, or at least was 20, 30 years ago.  The post-war consumer society threw a high standard of living at people, hoping it would turn them into a better people.  But it didn't, it just made them decadent.
I've been calling this hypothesis  "Boomers were the first Universal Basic Aristocracy."
So now we're in the process of losing that standard of living, and we're none too happy about it, but we can't remember what came before.
There's a parallel question: "Can moral problems be solved with technological means?"

To me the answer seems to be "No", but maybe that's because when a society succeeds at solving a moral problem with technology, that just looks like *absence* of a moral problem.  Not SOLVED moral problem.
There are many ways technology has alleviated formerly-degrading poverty and privation, and it's not *all* a matter of sweeping it under the rug to faraway low-wage countries.
Although some of it is.  And how could you, end-beneficiary, ever possibly know what those fractions are?
The more visible effect of technology on our moral and spiritual condition is that technologies tempt us with easy harm.  Occasions of sin.
Every new technology creates a new moral & spiritual race to the bottom.  "Everyone else is using the cheap trick, so I have to too."
This is how most people *experience* technological progress, while the new wealth that's created is largely invisible, and taken for granted.

Failure modes usually fall under two categories.
1.   Trash can has been hidden because it's considered "gross."
2.  There are other open containers competing for attention, "which one is the trash can?"  Recycling, scrap metal, bucket of unsorted junk, etc.
Occasionally you find a third:  the space is so new or infrequently used that it's never been considered as a "room" in its own right, so assumed not to "deserve" its own trash can.

Especially common in spaces designated for storage.  People think "I won't spend any time in here, I won't do any work here."  Which is wrong because those spaces end up serving as "buffer zones" where people put stuff they're in denial about needing to throw away.

Lack of a trash can perpetuates the stuckness.  You stand in that room agonizing over throwing something away, and then when you do decide to pull the trigger, there's no trash can!  So back on the shelf it goes ... to languish until eventual storage bankruptcy.

Empowered Techno-Pessimism:
The newer version of anything is usually worse.

It's worse because they've made it more complicated, added poorly-thought out gimmicks, chiseled away at the core functionality, used cheaper materials, and optimized the product for advertising over functionality.
But ...
We can undo the bullshit by slashing and cutting it off—it is our right and our duty as people with taste to simply chop off the superfluous parts from the new versions and throw them in the trash.
Most things can be made better by making them simpler, and that is relatively easy to do, actually.  Easier than building from scratch.

@RadicalRasmalai I've been seeing a lot of things through the lens of widespread narcissism lately.  I was particularly intrigued by these highlights from nosilverv's thread: https://t.co/pu8pwavBE2

@RadicalRasmalai One hallmark of narcissism that I picked up from Nancy McWilliams is "reflexive de-valuing."
https://t.co/s4BJ1IqMOC
@nealjclark1 What is folk culture?

@RadicalRasmalai I'm still chewing on this, I think there's a lot of facets to it.

1.  It can be used to reinforce a sense of superiority.  "Sure, I could've done that."

@RadicalRasmalai 2.  Perfectionism.  If a kid feels a parent *needs* them to be perfect, they learn to suppress the parts of themselves that don't conform to that image of perfection.  "Reflexive de-valuing" of anything not perfect is how that happens.  Applies both to oneself and others.

@RadicalRasmalai One angle I think is pretty clearly central and true is:

“Narcissism is what happens when you try to heal a relational wound without an Other to relate to.”
@RadicalRasmalai So instead of relating to real people, who can relate back to you, you relate to images, recordings, or yourself.  Facsimiles of an Other.
@RadicalRasmalai The media environment *loves it* when we do this.  It feeds off it.

@RadicalRasmalai Since visual media is superstimuli: curated selections of the most attractive people saying the most interesting things, set to music, you get to feel like the “Other” you’re “relating” to in media is superior to the real life others you might relate to genuinely.

@RadicalRasmalai So you can turn that reflexive de-valuing towards real people, who you measure against the standards of fictional characters, to *defend* against the way they might relate to you in a way that fails to conform to your narcissistic self-image.
@RadicalRasmalai Dismissing their opinion because they're not ideal enough, basically.

@RadicalRasmalai Seems like reflexive de-valuing of your own achievements functions to keep you on a treadmill, maintain hope, and ... since the achievements were only made by imitating an external image, they didn't come from *you* and therefore don't mean anything.

@RadicalRasmalai That's why the feeling of having achieved a goal never arrives.

Okay it seems like anti-commodification sentiment is having a moment.  We’ve been living under universal commodified everything our entire lives, and we’re sick of it. https://t.co/BGW89lG0rM https://t.co/AS9jnoefzW
We hate it, but we know no other way.  What does non-commodified existence even look like?

How can we even *survive* without commodities?  We eat commodities for our daily bread.  The roof over our heads is made from commodity lumber and commodity shingles.  The clothes on our back are made from commodity threads.

OK, I’m only going to gesture at this, because it’s a slow process.  The essence begins, in your mind, with considering your trading partners as entities worthy of respect.  I dare say worthy of dignity.
You can still use money, you have to.  You are still buying things on the open market.  All you’re doing, for now, is making a decision to not be purely mercenary, every time.

Because that’s the essence of commodification: interchangeability.  “Doesn’t matter where it comes from, it’s all the same.  Because it’s been standardized.  So I might as well get the cheapest.  How do I get the most for the least?”
And you can get away with that.  Amazon doesn’t require you to extend them respect, but they won’t offer you respect either.  You’re both using each other.  And you let it happen.

No, it does matter where things come from.  Everybody knows this in their bones.  We’ve been denying that knowledge for a long time and we feel weird about it.  But for the most part we shrug and carry on.
Now, I you might be saying: “Okay, sure, I can treat the STORE with respect, but what about where THEY got it from?  EVERY purchase eventually traces back to child labor and Foxconn suicide nets!”
Nah, that’s intellectualizing and catastrophizing.  You need to stop that.

Just pay attention to who you’re dealing with.  How does the deal make you feel?  Does it feel … upstanding?  Does it feel … shameful?  Would you want to deal with them again?  It doesn’t have to be perfect.

It’s an expectation that there could be *some* quality involved in transactions, besides money.  I don’t know what it is yet.  But you have to begin with space in your attention—there might be something there.

To withstand decadence is a particular and subtle strength.
Americana rests on a bedrock of hardship.  Charismatic mega-hardship.
The hardship of a commodities powerhouse nation.  That's what we are, or used to be.
The commodities came from the blood and sacrifice of the people.  And that was a source of pride.
Now that the commodities come mostly from the machines, the pride is lost.
What made the hardship mega-charismatic was the labor and the danger of it. https://t.co/SpqjrOhPHi
Since globalization happened, and American labor declined, America has a new hardship.
A hardship we don't know how to name yet.  And so it is a hardship of despair.
It's a hardship that doesn't feel noble to endure.
A hardship of Universal Basic Decadence.  That's what the consumer society was.
A hardship of a propagandized people—lost and confused and isolated, fed on substitutes.
A people traumatized deliberately, so as to render them better market participants.
Decadence causes problems.  Big problems.  Diseases.  Spiritual maladies.  Sin, corruption, despair, death.
Certain aristocracies developed cultural modes for mitigating these decadence problems.
Elegant cruelty in homeopathic doses.
America, for the first time, is experiencing universal decadence.  The middle class was like "What if we had an entire nation of aristocrats?  Wouldn't that be grand?"

And it happened, and mostly nobody noticed it, nobody appreciated it, because everyone's attention was turned upwards, towards ever more acquisition, ever more climbing and striving.  They couldn't see what they had.
And in the meantime, the decadence problems set in.  A newfangled decadence, unrecognizable as such because it appeared lower class.
So now we are finding out if any middle class cultural immune responses to decadence will emerge.
The middle class is turning over, coders are the new middle class, the old professions are dying.
The new labor class is the service industry.  Delivery is as much a need as ever.
We need a way to labor effectively despite the demoralizing hardship of decadence.
Labor in the absence of material scarcity.  Labor in the absence of need.
Labor in the absence of the bosses.  Labor of one's own volition.

Every bit of the real economy is either cute and tiny, or unfathomably huge and uncontrollable and probably evil.

Like organic CSA farms.  They're cute and fake compared to factory farms because their output is so tiny, even though it's jewel-like and perfect.  It's got so much more human attention per unit output, and rich people love it, but it can't make a dent compared to Tyson Chicken.

The schism exists because they're operating by different logics.  The tiny organic farm is basically operating ideologically.  The factory farm is operating on spreadsheet brain, plus maybe some mafia logic.
That's the producer side schism, but the consumer schism is just as large.
For the middle class to survive, it will have to reconcile the logics of operating both inside and outside this commodity economy, which is fundamentally alien and inhuman in its scale.
You can't evaluate your daily life activities by comparing them to commodity production.  And you can't hold commodity production to warm and fuzzy human ethical standards.
But people try, in both directions, and the result seems to mostly be GUILT.

We haven't exactly managed to raise Dunbar's Number.  What we've done is ... structured life so you pass through a series of Dunbar sets. https://t.co/9BFi2SsWtt
Family as your first Dunbar set.  Grade school as your second, high school as your third, college as your fourth, grad school as your fifth.  Change cities a few times, new Dunbar set each time.
By this time you can't recount the earlier sets from memory, but they would come back to you if you ran into them.  Think about, say, a high school graduation class photo.
This creates a large class of sort of strange relationships, that are close-but-not-loyal.  "Common culture" relationships, as distinct from friendships.
There's a "mode" you go into, a way of relating and conversing, an easy shorthand.  You might fall into it at a party, but you'd feel no need keep in touch afterwards.

Pay attention to the emotional valence when money changes hands.
"The modern economy is primarily composed of things and services available for money, ratcheting to allow fewer and fewer non-monetary costs."
OK that's a perfect distillation of one true and accurate blackpill perspective that I am trying to counter.
The counter is: only by recognizing things that are non-fungible can you escape that ratcheting noose.
There are parallel worlds, parallel economies, hiding in plain sight, and you can't see them from within the consumer value system.
By their nature they *try* to remain invisible because, consciously or not, they recognize commodification as a caustic solvent.
In order to approach them and trade with them, you have to come correct. And that means proposing a fair exchange of non-fungible goods.

You have been hoodwinked by the consumer value system into thinking that "only legible transactions can be ethical. The transparency makes them fair; the fungibility means that nobody gets preferential treatment."
What if the opposite were true? What if the *obligation* to trade with *anyone* is an unfair burden to place on any finite being?
What if trade becomes *more* ethical when it happen between parties with history, not less?
There's a fine line between cronyism and alliance. Maybe you just have to trust your own judgement about it.

@natural_hazard I'm trying to find an amazing youtube video I saw a while back.  Exactly along these lines.  Driving me nuts that I can't find it.  Came across it on hackernews, I think.

@natural_hazard The guy hooked up a whole bunch of electrical sensors along a big open circuit with a bunch of different "prongs," or paths   He then traced what happened when he connected it to ... I think a steady state DC voltage from a controllable power source.

@natural_hazard Looking at the sensor readings in slow motion, it turned out that the voltage acted like a water wave traveling through a maze in a wave pool.  Like the voltage traveled along the wires to the end, realized they couldn't complete the circuit, and "bounced" back ...

@natural_hazard ... causing very short-lived spikes in certain places that exceeded the voltage being generated by the source.  That was of interest to circuit designers because it showed how higher voltages than they were expecting might happen under certain switching on/off conditions.

@natural_hazard That's the one!!!  Thank you this was driving me nuts!  Also a weird experience of doubting my memory like "Wait was that actually a guy making a fluid model with a wave pool or something?"
